replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'll start with the usual caveat, that human beings (myself very much included) aren't very smart, and in particular we have about as much chance of understanding the gods as the dust mites in Mozart's wig had of understanding his music. All we have are guesses based on our limited experience of gods; the word "theology" literally means "talking about gods," nothing more serious than that.

With that in mind, I'll offer the following. First, YHVH is probably derived from an archaic form of the Hebrew verb "to be;" "He who Is" is probably a good workable translation. (Weirdly, there's another etymology from a verb meaning "to fall;" the Gnostics who believed that YHVH was a fallen being may have been riffing off that.) So it's not a name, it's a title -- rather like the English "God" -- and it's far from certain that there's only one deity behind the title. Certainly the behavior of the various religions that all claim to call on that one god suggest that there are a lot of different Jehovim out there. The ancient Greeks used to use Theos to mean "a god," or "the god," or "whichever god might happen to show up" -- I suspect this is a more accurate usage than strict monotheism allows for.

The same is emphatically true of Christ. Read portrayals of Christ from a dozen different Christian churches and you're very clearly dealing with a dozen different gods. The Christ of my Calvinist ancestors was a pale, wrathful lord of death passing terrible judgment on the living; his namesake among the Quakers and Shakers was a gentle spirit of universal love. These are not the same beings.

So one part of the answer to your question is that it depends on which YHVH and which Christ you have in mind -- and another part is that those two are not distinct from "all the other gods," but are labels by which an assortment of gods have been known.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My answer is, indeed, "yes." Though one should keep JMG's answer in mind: if you're dealing with a deity like the Calvinist one, the answer would be a "no." Based on your description of a loving deity, I don't think you're being contacted by one of those Jesii or Jehovim. I can tell you what I've done and my own working theory, as far as worship and devotion go.

My background is primarily Roman Catholic. Most of the time, I've used the Catholic distinction between "latria" and "dulia." Latria is the highest form of worship, and is therefore restricted to the highest God (whatever his/her/its actual name is). In Catholicism, that deity is known as the Holy Trinity. Other powers, including other gods, archangels, angels, saints, local spirits, etc., are offered (by me, that is) dulia worship. It is a form of veneration and honor to establish and maintain good relations. The differences between latria and dulia are largely internal to the worshiper. In practice, there is little visible difference. In Catholicism, the sacrifice of the Mass is the only purely latreutical worship reserved for "The God" alone.

There is one thing to note about Deuteronomy and "no other gods before me." A deity called YHWH established a covenant with the ancient Israelites. Picking up on JMG's comment, some scholars think this is a title of the Canaanite high god, El, in his role as the creator of the heavenly armies. Frank Moore Cross has an excellent book that argues for this possibility. At any rate, whoever this god originally was, as part of that covenant, the Israelites were obligated to henotheistic worship of him. That covenant applied to the Israelites, not to Gentiles. Unless you're also Jewish, I'm not sure what relevance this law, in its original literal sense of henotheism, would have to you. Christians, including Catholics, usually interpret the "Old Law" in the moral sense of its import (more of the spirit of the law, you might say). At least in Catholicism, the spirit of this law has resulted in the distinction between different types of worship and obligation (i.e. latria, dulia, etc.).

Of course, you might get a different take from others. But the above is a result of my own experience and my own research into the matter.

There are various ways of talking about the planes of being. The Cos.Doc. uses the version from Theosophy, which is in order:

Upper Spiritual
Lower Spiritual
Upper Mental
Lower Mental
Upper Astral
Lower Astral
Physical (including etheric)

comments.content: When separating the coarse from the subtle in plant alchemy, the coarse ends up in the compost bin. Where does the coarse go when doing internal alchemy?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you're getting rid of the coarse in plant alchemy, according to everything I've learned, you're doing it wrong. The coarse -- meaning here the solid plant matter left behind when the volatile fractions are extracted from it -- is properly calcinated to ash, the ash finely powdered and reduced to its pure mineral salts, and then combined with the subtle fractions in the final magistery. Solve et coagula -- and the final coagula must bring together all the components of the original plant, or it fails.

The same is true with internal alchemy. You separate the subtle from the coarse, not for the purpose of disposing of the coarse, but so that each can be purified and raised in vibration before being brought back together in a new unity.

replies.title: Re: Divination question; seeing “things” around the house
replies.content:
1) Gauging the intensity of any prediction is one of the difficult aspects of any divination, and it's something that most beginners and many intermediate students reliably get wrong, usually by overstatement. My working rule for beginners is that such things are never as drastic as you think. "Oh, dread, it's the Death card, I'm going to die!" No, it means that you're going to go through some kind of minor change in your life. Practice helps, and so does the habit of revisiting your divinations after the fact.

2) The first thing to do in a situation like this is to make sure that it's not a medical issue, as Methylethyl has suggested. My guess is that it's a common effect of magical practice -- you're beginning to sense the inner planes, and your brain isn't quite sure how to process that, so you get astral static of various kinds; I had phantom cats, for example. If that's what it turns out to be, don't worry about it; it should go away in a few months, as your brain gets used to astral and etheric perceptions.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't have much to offer on relations with Hephaestus other than to consider prayer and/or divination to work out what role He might need to play (or not) in your life, but a few thoughts on getting back to fitness after an injury (around which I have more experience than I might wish):

First off, mobility-limiting injuries can really crush your sense of competence and autonomy, especially if they come after you've gotten very fit. If you're feeling that way, it's not unusual at all, so don't beat yourself up. On the other hand, that feeling can sometimes lead to a desire to get back to working out as soon as possible, which can be risky: if you start back in on what you were doing before, you can re-hurt yourself, which slows the recovery down even more.

Depending on the severity of the injury, you might consider working with a trained physical therapist - they'll help you do what you can without re-injuring yourself. If that's not the right choice for you, consider looking for some easier/lower-impact exercises that you can do to ease back into the swing of things. I tweaked something in my back picking up my daughter late last year, and I figured out it was because I had been pushing too hard on deadlifts without enough work on my core, so when I felt ready to start working out again, I began with some very gentle yoga and stretches, then moved on to the Five Rites, working up gently as described in the original pamphlet. Besides that, stretching and massage can also have a much bigger effect on soft tissue healing than most folks realize.

Good luck with everything, and I hope some of this helps!
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) Fate and destiny, as I use the words, are two different things. Fate is the collected consequences of your past actions -- it's identical to the Indian concept of karma. Destiny is the direction your innermost self is going. Those can be in harmony, in conflict, or unrelated to each other.

2) In some cases, yes. If you were born male in 1895 anywhere in Europe, for example, your destiny and fate led straight to the carnage of the First World War. In other cases? It really varies.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Emerald Tablet was probably written by a Greek alchemist sometime in the late Roman period, though the first surviving copy is Arabic and dates from the 9th century AD. Yes, there was plenty of understanding of occultism before its time? As for Egypt, it was certainly an important source for occult ideas, especially in the western world, but it was never the only game in town.

In Atlantean times, if occult traditions are anything to go by, occultism was well understood but it wasn't widely spread -- the cultures of the Atlantean age are said to have been much more hierarchical than ours, and most kinds of knowledge -- even the sort of thing you get in high school these days -- were only available to initiates of the various temples. Their advanced technologies were equally restricted to the initiated elite; imagine a society in which the common people lived in medieval conditions while the initiates of the temples had access to radio, aircraft, and the like. Ours is very democratic by contrast!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
>the initiates of the temples had access to radio, aircraft, and the like
Whoa, wait a minute! What?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's one of the claims of the old traditions: that some of the late civilizations of Atlantean times had technologies comparable to ours in some ways -- devices for communicating at a distance, devices for flying through the air, and so on -- but that these were solely the property of the temple priesthoods and were presented to the masses as miracles rather than technologies anyone could use. Then, after that age ended and the last of the civilizations fell, scraps of technology were preserved in various corners of the world until the last centers of the old priesthoods finally sank into decadence or were destroyed. Consider this ancient Persian image of divinity...

comments.title: "Joined" a church as a child
comments.content:
I was raised in the Methodist church. Oddly my parents were not religious, but for my mother the church community was a very important part of her social world. My dad resisted going, but my mom pretty much made us go to church every Sunday until we were too big to push around as easily. I felt even as a small child that the clergy were by and large stiff and pretentious, without warmth, or any genuine wisdom or spiritual insight. (No reflection on Methodism in general, just this particular suburban middle-class congregation.) When I was twelve or so I went through the confirmation process. I really didn’t have a choice in the matter. It involved going through some classes about the faith, and then a ceremony in front of the congregation on a Sunday where we formally joined the church as (almost) adults. I guess this is the follow up to baptism. I don’t recall what exactly I said or committed to in the ceremony (it was many decades ago) but I am certain I was instructed to make some kind of commitment to God to be part of his flock. At the time, I did it because it was expected of me, without any genuine conviction or desire, and after I was independent I have never been a church-goer in that or any other Christian denomination.
Lately though, I am wondering if that vow I made, however insincerely, is preventing me from really engaging in another path. I don’t dislike Christians in general, or the Christian God, it is just that I resonate much more with a polytheistic sense of the world. It is possible to renounce that vow to God I made as a child? How would I do that, and should I do that? I just want him to know that I respect him but I cannot commit to a God that will not permit worship of other deities. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: "Joined" a church as a child
replies.content: If you didn't make that vow out of genuine conviction or desire it isn't binding. You should, out of politeness, pray to the Christian god, tell him the whole story, let him know what you have in mind, thank him for whatever protection and blessings he might have given you, and then let the connection go.

replies.title: Re: Influences: Inner and Outer

replies.title: Re: Planes
replies.content:
That's why I differentiate between the material and etheric planes. If you do that, the seven sub-planes of the material plane work out as follows:

7th (lowest): solid matter
6th: liquid matter
5th: gaseous matter
4th: electromagnetism
3rd: gravity
2nd: weak nuclear force
1st (highest): strong nuclear force

In every set of sub-planes, the lower three have the nature of substance, the middle one has the nature of energy, and the upper three have the nature of space. It all works rather nicely. Then in the etheric plane the lower three sub-planes are the chemical ethers, the middle one is the life ether, and the three higher are the light ethers.

comments.title: Various
comments.content:
Salutations, JMG!

First something that struck me while walking through a leafy section of our local college; your theory about Neptune being assigned to Kether seems strengthened by the structure of astrology in general. Namely, the 12th house is normally assigned to Pisces on its cusp, and thus is ruled by Neptune. What is more Kether than the 12th house of mystery and the Unseen Realms! Not to mention, the 12th house is the final one before the Ascendant restarts the Zodiac, and that finality is similar to Kether's in the Cabala. If you already wrote this in the book, apologies, because then I missed it.

First question, have you considered what Neptune should be properly called, if the Greek mythological framework is upheld? I thought your suggestion of Prometheus for Uranus in a podcast appearance (and maybe in the Twliight of pluto, though I don't remember) was entirely appropriate. Have you had any idea about what Neptune should be called since then? I think a goddess of the sea would be extremely appropriate, but I can't think of any with the grandeur necessary for such a mystic and exalted influence. Unfortunately, all the Olympian goddesses are also very distinct from it.

Second, I was reading Sex and Civilization by Amaury de Riencourt. One of his hypotheses was that civilizations destroy themselves or stagnate by over-suppressing one of masculine and feminine energies, far, far more often the feminine. Therefore, feminism-esque movements for women to be equal in the public square (which apparently happened in Greece, Rome, and the Abbasid Caliphate) are both a cause and a symptom of decline, as it is basically a tacit acknowledgement that yang/masculinity is so overvalued that women seek to take on its roles rather than demanding respect for yin based ones. As a Uranian-dominant and a modern, this struck me as both a little terrifying, and quite apt given the evidence of women's unhappiness in modern society. Something I also thought about was the increase in social complexity ala Tainter; societies add complexity in response to crisis, creating economic but more importantly SOCIAL complexity, which women thrive at navigating. However, once women begin to have a definite advantage in this way, it may also be a marker that society has reached or will soon reach the point of diminishing returns for adding new systems or elaborating on previous ones. Social complexity is also a killer of traditional mores, which would explain both why women take on a more traditionally masculine way of being, and why they seem to excel at it once they do, much to the chagrin of men.

Okay, this is long, but the point is, I realized that more of the planets were masculine than feminine after the discovery of the five new ones in the age of civilization's beginning, whereas before that, it was just Sun and Moon, one masculine one feminine. The Sun, Mercury-sort of, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn are Masculine, while the Moon, Venus, and Neptune are feminine. Could this be the reason why civilizations tend to become over-masculine as they evolve? (note, these questions in this paragraph are all rhetorical) Could this imbalance of astrological influences multiply over time, causing the Saturnine, Jovial, Solar, and Martial influences to express themselves with further and further strength as civilizations create grand superstructures? Could the very creation and dramatic collapse of those superstructures, the flaming out of civilizational supernovas, be a reflection of a cosmic imbalance that prioritizes the influence of the masculine in a vaccuum, but inevitably leads to overreach because of its dominating presence? Is a sort of grand, long-lasting version of "combustion", the state of the sun burning away another planet's influence, the necessary cosmic balance of energies to produce civilization? I wonder if this some sort of terrible lesson for us to learn as a species, that imbalance does not pay in the long term, or if is a temporary period of flux where the masculine exhausts itself, and the feminine is released as a rejuvenating force and that dominates another long era of human existence. Perhaps the winding-down period of the human species will either have as its beginning the discovery of two new feminine planets, or one or two masculine planets will be destroyed by a terrible cosmic disaster that also wrecks the earth's ability to support civilization in an even more permanent way than climate change.

Okay, this is too long, haha. Feel free to ignore it, your time is valuable after all. That said, if you do end up reading it, do you have any thoughts about the purpose or effects of this grand cosmic imbalance that coincidentally seemed to present itself right when civilization came about? Do you think this will ever change, or is humanity stuck with a rather lopsided series of energies to work with for the rest of our existence?

-Derpherder

replies.title: Re: Various
replies.content:
1) I had that well in mind, though I didn't put it in the book. My nominee for a proper name for Neptune is Rhea the Titaness, whose title is "Mother of the Gods" and whose name may mean something like "She who Flows."

2 etc.) That's a fascinating speculation. My guess is that as our species matures, some of the planets will be assigned different genders. I could see the Sun, Mars, Jupiter, and Prometheus/Uranus as the male planets, the Moon, Venus, Demeter/Saturn and Rhea/Neptune as the female planets, and Mercury as the ambivalent and ambisexual mediator among them: that would be a stable arrangement, energetically speaking.

replies.title: Re: Various
replies.content:
She who flows! Magnificent! I love it. Also, the reassignment is apt, I didnt think of that but should have!

-Derpherder
